reviews.review_text:
I ended up adoring this game. It's got flaws, it's got jank, but if you're willing to put up with that and apply a LITTLE bit of modding to smooth out the edges you're in for an experience Bethesda WISHED they could make. Even if you find this time period only MILDLY interesting, you'll get sucked in to a (mostly) historically accurate journey. 

Here's are some recommended mods for a "vanilla+" experience: Bushes Only Collision Remover. Easy to See Herbs. First Person Herb Picking. Quicksave. Stay Clean Longer Get Dirty Gradually. Unlimited Saving NSHL.

At the point of typing this I'm stuck on the Nest Of Vipers quest, which I feel isn't really far in the game. I gave up on this game originally when I first bought it because the "tutorial" frustrated me beyond reason, and I had just come off playing other medieval games like Mount and Blade, and Mordhau, I thought maybe I was just stuck in a mode that didn't favor KCD1's style of gameplay, so I put it off. My latest experience has gone a little more tolerably, but only a little. I am trying to let it grow on me, but I do not see myself finishing this game. I don't really even see myself finishing this quest. Usually my ego kicks in at a point like this and I force myself to play a game because maybe I'm just bad and I just need to getgud, but unlike soulsborne games or other medieval combat games, I feel absolutely nothing for the idea of getting "good enough" to finish KCD1.

The combat is truly an awful experience. Interesting concept, HORRIBLE application. They teach you how to do attack combos, block, parry, do feints, and the combat wheel is cool and all, but all the mechanics are designed for one-on-one combat, which is non-applicable to most of the scenarios you will find yourself in if you pursue the main quest. Furthermore, and most importantly: Any enemy that isn't a threadbare, half-dead bandit will "master strike" you to death if you try to attack them normally. Everything you learned about fighting in KCD1 can be tossed out the window the moment you learn master strike, because it is the only way to win battles.
 I'm not even being hyperbolic, the enemy NPC's will absolutely slaughter you if you try attacks and do combos, and not just spam master strikes instead. Master strikes are a timed, single button press "parry and strike" attack that is unblockable, and primed by an attacker swinging their weapon at the user. Essentially, you learn to stop attacking, and just wait for enemies to attack you, because there's no point to doing anything else.

The combat camera is probably the final boss of every battle. It doesn't help you in the slightest. It'll even help your pursuers cut you down if you try to run away from a battle you might not have wanted.

I just don't get it, It's like the devs just spent their time looking at "this game is too easy" posts on reddit and just made the player experience more and more awful until only a modder or a masochist could enjoy this game. I can't even get mods to work (yet?)

Also, Henry doesn't know what to do with slightly raised surfaces, like fallen trees buried part way into the ground, or the side of ramps.

The story and open world is kind of nice, I'll admit..

Buy on sale.

---{ Summary }---
Honestly, there are a couple of things you should know before starting Kingdom Come: Deliverance.

First of all, KCD1 feels like both a complete game and half of a larger story at the same time. While Henry's journey in this game is satisfying on its own, to fully experience and appreciate his story, you'll eventually want to continue with Kingdom Come: Deliverance II.

The combat system can feel strange and even frustrating at first. It takes time to understand and get comfortable with its mechanics, especially if you're coming from more traditional RPGs. However, once it clicks, it becomes surprisingly engaging and rewarding.

The story is grounded, realistic, and consistently entertaining. It has its fair share of serious moments, but there are also plenty of humorous and memorable interactions that help bring the world to life. One thing players should keep in mind is that this is not a typical power fantasy. Even near the end of the game, a group of armed peasants can still pose a real threat if you're careless. Many players who quit early do so because they expect a more traditional power-scaling system, but Kingdom Come: Deliverance is committed to realism first and foremost.

Overall, this is a highly enjoyable RPG that can easily provide 50+ hours of content, especially when you include the three major DLCs. Its story, progression, and combat are very different from what most modern RPGs offer, which means it may not be everyone's cup of tea. However, I genuinely believe everyone should at least give it a try. Sometimes the best way to expand your gaming palate is to experience something completely different, and Kingdom Come: Deliverance is one of the best examples of that.

It's absolutely a game for some, just not for me. There are too many poor design descisions for me to fully enjoy this game. Too many moments that I became frustraited and annoyed at the absurdity that this game sometimes demands of you. The game performs okay but can chug in some parts.

The game is unapologetically it's self. I can get used to the combat and a variety of other different mechanics but there are something that bother me to no end. I have issues with master strikes in combat and how they are unfair both ways, I hate how extremely tedious and boring the quests and tasks are, I hate I have to go to the baths and clean myself constantly. There are also many aspects of the game I found really fun. The stealing, the sneaking, the fighting (master strikes excluded), the exploring. There's just not enough here of good quality and ease of access to reccomend. Too tedious and frustraiting for me.

UPDATE: Just finished the game, or so I thought. I'm now being forced to finish the Hans DLC before being able to finish the game. Forcing me to complete a DLC I have zero interest in just to finish the game? What a joke.

3/10

I've always been a big fan of RPG games—particularly The Elder Scrolls series and the Fallout series. Those two series are my gateway into the realm of RPG. But in each of those game, I've always sought to increase the realism of the game through mods. I've always valued immersion in my playthroughs. Just 5 days ago I decided to purchase Kingdom Come: Deliverance. And safe to say, this game has elevated my standards in RPG. Its complex combat mechanics forces you to improvise depending on the situation. That makes the combat extremely enjoyable and there would still be room to experiment with finding the best way to beat your enemies even until the late game. Side activities that are not bare-bones, complex weapon maintenance mechanics, complex stealth mechanics, complex alchemy mechanics, those are just the starting point because personally, even I haven't been able to explore all the features of this game yet. I would absolutely recommend RPG enthusiasts—and for those who enjoy realism—to purchase this game.

A good game with a great attempt at realism. At first it seems very confusing, and sometimes you don't understand what to do. The plot is pretty good as well as some side quests (Kudos to the developers for allowing us to complete quests in different ways). The combat system is quiet poorly implemented, and you die a lot at first. Archery is also difficult; without a crosshair, it's not entirely clear where the arrow will land (but you can revert it by a single console command). The interaction mechanics are very realistic (whether it is alchemy or sharpening a sword on a grindstone, etc.). I also didnt like save system so infinite save mod ftw!

Father Godwin is probably the best character in the game. Too bad he had little interaction time but his main quest is probably the best quest in whole game.

Looking forward to play KCD 2.

Kingdom Come: Deliverance is a game that should be considered an industry standard for single player games. Its clear that Warhorse Studios was dedicated to making this game a success. I highly encourage anyone who enjoys single player games to give this game a try.

It seems like a nice medieval concept at the beginning but I am really annoyed all of these bugs. This game is literally unplayable and waste of time. I deleted this game 5 or 6 times and installed it again. The thing is I want to finish what I started (as in other things) and end this pain. I am not recommending this game in this current state.

Is the game Fun? Yes

Did I have to look up a guide for almost every quest ever? Yes

Would I recommend? Only if you are fine with looking stuff up every 5 seconds, which I am not.

For player of RPG games there are only two worlds - before KCD and after KCD, because after you will finish these games (KCD and KCD2) there are no other game quite like them and you will always want more...

I bought  first KCD game long after it was published and started playing it even longer after. In fact I had interupted playing AC game series and started first KCD game and it was like someone splashed you with cold water. After you are practically a sniper wíth the bow in AC Origins and demigod with melee weapons in AC Oddyssea, the KCD will bring you back to reality. You’ll find out, what is it like to be only a human who can barely draw a bow and can't hit a barn and who returns from sparring with wooden swords battered and exhausted!

First, you need to learn how to play the game itself—the rules, the mechanics, and the fact that you never get anything for free. Then your main character, Henry, has to learn everything from scratch to become a hero from a nobody, a grown man from a boy, and a mighty knight from a blacksmith’s son. And the path to achieving that is incredibly difficult, it’s challenging, it requires patience, it demands hard work, and it’s about survival - to know when to run and hide or to stand against your enemy and fight.

The life in middle ages was tough and you’ll really get a sense of just how hard it was. You’ll feel the fear when you realize your enemies—those terrifying-looking Cumans—are right behind you, that you could be robbed and killed on the road by bandits, that there are limits to how much weight you can carry and how much stamina you have, that you need to eat, sleep, and take care of your belongings, and really think through how you’ll spend your hard-earned silver groschen.
And unlike in other RPG games (most of which are fantasy, surreal, or futuristic), in the KCD world there is no magic, no supernatural force, or overpowered weapon to help you.

But after you will be patient and your Henry will learn how to really handle himself than you’ll truly enjoy every hour spent in 15th-century Bohemia. You’ll be rewarded with a rich narrative that encompasses not only the main characters but also the stories of the many people from that era whom you’ll meet during your adventurous journey in autentic and historically accurate surroundings and in impresive natural landscape created not only for exploration but even for just walking and enjoying it.

So for not writing about the superlatives of this game only, I will also mention some of the criticized features: 
Inventory and changing clothes and gear—there are many types of loot, materials, clothes, and gear you can obtain, it might seem chaotic or confusing at first, but you’ll get used to it over time. It’s a shame, though, that you have to equip  every single item in your inventory separately - especially changing clothes and gear from casual wear to combat gear every time you encounter some bad guys, which is a bit annoying. But after a few hours, you’ll be doing it so automatically that you might not even notice (luckily, in KCD2, this problem is solved with a set of pre-selected outfits).
Collision with bushes – even relatively small bushes or vegetation act like a wall, and you can’t get through them, even on horseback. This might be a little frustrating, but it’s no reason to just quit the game. I believe there’s a mod for the game that fixes this little bush problem.
Combat system - it has its own rules and is complicated and hard to master, but as you level up, you’ll learn to adapt and find your own style for defeating enemies. You can get by with just the basics (I’m no master swordsman) throughout the entire game—no cheats needed. The only console command I used was to display the fire cursor. My best advice for those who quit? Train, train, and train, and complete all possible side quests before continuing with the main story, and you won’t have any trouble with constantly dying.

Overall, Kingdom Come: Deliverance is a unique gaming experience -one that leaves a powerful, lasting impression of something you’ve experienced firsthand, a story you’ve personally lived through, and the choices you’ve made, all set in the real world of 1403 in the Kingdom of Bohemia.
What makes this game great is that very sense of reality. That reality makes the story more believable, more intense, more addictive -simply more...
And once you’ve finished it, you’ll want to know what comes next, how the story will continue; you’ll always want more...

Overall a masterpiece of RPGs. It is brutal and rough at the beginning with the combat and exploration when you find Cumans while moving through towns. But once you manage to overcome that, the story is just beautiful. It has political and church drama, love, crazy side stories like [spoiler] terrorizing a town that there's a ghost [/spoiler]. The immersion on the realism on the game, like making potions, blacksmith, is just incredible. Something you need to experience.
The game is amazing. It is also borderline unplayable (at least for me). Got every bug in the book and then some, including random crashes that wonderfully align with the saving system

There's a very good game here, but just be warned, it is buried under an absolute mountain of jank and bad design. I am no stranger to jank (some of my favorite games are full of it!) but this harkens back to the glory days of Daggerfall. That said, it is pretty damn stable - I had maybe two crashes in 40+ hours of gameplay - so it's a massive improvement on that front. As for the terrible design decisions, the good news is the game has been out for many years, and there's a vibrant mod community, so you can mod out most of the rough edges. There's still plenty left, but it helps a great deal. 

But again, once you get past that (if you can) this is a pretty good game! The setting is unique, and has an excellent sense of place, and the storytelling is pretty solid. 

Recommended, with some major caveats.
Great story even with challenging sword fights in the beginning,` the rest including graphics, humor, side quests are making it top game!

Kingdom Come: Deliverance is honestly one of the most immersive RPGs I’ve ever played.

This isn’t a fantasy game where you become an overpowered hero in 20 minutes. You start as basically nobody  and that’s exactly what makes the journey feel so rewarding. Every sword fight, every piece of armor, every coin earned actually matters.

The world feels alive. Forests are dense and atmospheric, villages feel believable, and the attention to medieval realism is insane. Combat can feel difficult at first, but once it clicks, it becomes incredibly satisfying. You actually need to learn timing, positioning, stamina management, and preparation instead of button mashing.

The storytelling is also surprisingly strong. Characters feel human, quests often have multiple outcomes, and even side quests can become memorable adventures. The game doesn’t hold your hand much, which may frustrate some players, but for others it creates one of the most rewarding RPG experiences out there.

What really impressed me was the immersion:

* no magical nonsense
* realistic medieval life
* believable NPC routines
* amazing atmosphere
* incredible soundtrack
* beautiful countryside

You genuinely feel like you’re living in 1403 Bohemia.

Yes, the game has some jank here and there, and combat takes patience to master, but if you give it time, Kingdom Come: Deliverance becomes something special.

This is not just an RPG.
It’s a medieval experience.

Highly recommended for players who love immersion, realism, exploration, and slow-burn progression.
This is a great game full of history lessons. I have never felt like a game is so alive as in the KCD games. Truly great

Absolute cinema. Okay but in all seriousness, love the storyline, love the animations, love the cinematics, and I love everything about this game. I love medieval games. I have played Half Sword a lot.

the game is very good and realistic, you can`t rush things you need to chill and learn how to fight, how to pickpocket, how to do poisons and more. the combat alone carries this game, i really like how the combat works and how you need to focus on the enemies one by one while watching the surrounding area, and the skills in the game are great, there are plenty of skills to learn, the way you level up skills is even greater, to level a skill you just use it, so you start as a weak beta who can`t carry a bow properly to becoming Hanzo, there is plenty of main and side quests to do, a lot of heretics to kill, and many places to visit, the graphics are super good for a 2018 game, there are plenty of armour and clothing pieces to wear, a lot of houses and churches to steal from, you can steal everything and anything in this game even the monastery! what a cool game design.


negatives: 
glitches: too many to count, it ruined a lot of my progress, i rage quit it a lot because of it.

saving: the saving is one of the most frustrating things in the game can`t save whenever you want, you need a special item to save or to go to your main bed and sleep to save, i hate it a lot but i got used to it after like 20 hours.

locking on: i hate the lock on system in the game when you fight more than one enemy, it will switch a lot between them making it hard to dodge or block attacks making you lose the fight.

time skip: too slow.


overall: good game, amazing experiance, worth to buy 8.5/10
Very immersive and fun, a bit hard sometimes, and probably my favourite game right now!

I truly hated history classes because of the burned-out history teachers who only showed up to collect their salary and had no passion for the subject or, if they did, they somehow managed to give the most boring lectures of all time. I was literally staring at the walls because they were more interesting. If they had managed to present historical events even 5% as well as this game did, history would have been my favorite subject. What a masterpiece of a game.
The perfect slow burn game. I don't think I'm even 10% in yet but I am loving every single thing about this game.
Fantastic once you get the hang of its very enjoyable, great game, ready for part 2 lets go!

Henry is a good lad, and that’s all you really need to know.

This is the kind of game you need to give time to. The combat system is very unique and can feel frustrating at first, but once you understand how it works, you’ll either love it or hate it. Personally, I found it extremely interesting and different from most RPGs.

This is not just another generic medieval-inspired game. You actually feel immersed in the world while also learning a bit about the history of the Bohemia region, which is now part of the Czech Republic.

Sometimes NPCs won’t trade with you because they are not in the correct spot or following their daily routine. Just wait for them to automatically return to their workplace and try talking to them again.

There are a few small bugs here and there, but nothing that takes away from the incredible level of detail and immersion this game offers.

The movement can feel a bit clunky at times, especially when walking or riding through bushes and uneven terrain. You can get stuck pretty easily, so it’s something you’ll eventually get used to.

Awesome RPG / action mix. Great graphics, great playability. The only downside, and a big one, is its combat, which is absolutely awful and confusing. Apart from that, one of the best medieval games out there.

Kingdom Come: Deliverance is one of the most immersive and enthralling games I have ever played. 

Starting off as a peasnant with nearly no skills and bad at almost everything and needing to slowly & realistically work your way into being competent at anything. Combat can be jank and very challenging while surronded by multiple enemies but this difficulty feels extremely rewarding when you get the hang of it.

Characters & their interactions are some of my favorite aspects in this game leading into some of the most intriguing and fun quests I've experienced in an rpg.

Saving I feel is the part of the game that I had the most tension with considering you need to use an item to save. I've heard multiple people complain about this aspect but I found that in long sessions it makes you think some things through and not just save scum constantly, love or hate this aspect I think it adds to the charm to the game, especially since I usually did large game sessions so would just use the save & quit option to end my session or save via sleeping in a bed to have Henry become rested. 

Overall I believe this game has a ton of soul and even if not for everyone is an amazing experience for those that aren't turned off by the game's learning curves.

What a game , one of the best games i ever played ,  just buy this masterpiece at any price and enjoy
I FEEL QUITE HUNGRY !!!

Now look, I tried to have my fun with this, don't regret the % buy but I'm honestly stunned. There was so much hype around this and its all mediocre at best, thrown together to form less then the sum of its parts with the genuine exception that is the the landscape. its presentation really left a strong impression and feels spot on. I'm no fan of realistic looks but here it works amazingly well 10/10

now about the combat, its really 2 systems stacked on top of each other (directional attacks and quick time events and they don't seem to interact well. I also don't see how this represents sword fighting in any meaningful way. maybe the animations are well researched!? In the end all challenge fades since you're greatest enemy is the camera. who would've thought? a classic really. more then 1 enemy = control gone. The design also commits the ultimate mistake of wrestling ones camera control away and shaking the view around which not only adds to the issue but is also known to be extremely frustrating to many.

I'll spare you the rest of my rant about random systems that don't work together well, harebrained controls and UI and all sorts of tech issues and odd priorities. I just wonder how projects that real effort and an army of man hours go into them, and the kind of game a lot of ppl are interested in, fail on basic aspects like this but it sure reeks of management issues, like game designers and writers didn't get to do their thing it seems. the later isn't bad, just quite trivial considering all the options. imagine bringing up the crusades and having nothing to say about them. and then there's plot armor for every nobleman, actually feudal. of course I want to slay those first. its core of the fantasy! it all feels very touristic - back to indie games

... ok the saving system, why does it even exist? Did anyone actually wonder or is it just something the boss wrote down on a napkin on day 1? couldn't be quiet about that one. Also bows don't even get perks unlike other weapons, minus spears which just don't exist. sorry. have a good day now.

A lot of bugs and issues that make the game less immersive
Main story is good and if I hadn't made a mistake and only completed the main quests, I would have liked the game much more

I wanted to give this game a chance, didn't mind it. Was going through story, had to fight a douche bag noble **AS PART OF STORY** then was immediately beaten to death by stupid ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ guards and had to **SIT THERE AND WAIT FOR AN UNSKIPPABLE CLOCK TO PASS 10 DAYS** like seriously? Not a single ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ game I've ever played has **EVER** had that garbage of a mechanic. Absolute ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ mechanic and buggy asf with arresting the player for fighting a forced fight. Like ♥♥♥♥ outta here.

Also I shouldn't even need to say this, out of any game I have EVER played, this game has the worst set of combat mechanics I have EVER seen. It doesn't matter how much you train, what skills you get, it doesn't ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ matter. It's ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. The devs shouldn't make anymore damn games. This is by far THE WORST GAME i've ever wasted money on.
A bit buggy, but I love the atmosphere and vibe of this game. Excellent story. Might a bit slow-paced for some, but if you like the immersion then it's perfect.

Quest markers don't lead you where they want you to go, the combat is straight up awful. randomly the game will pretend to be deep and have complex systems just to not care about something you assume they would. The random dogs some bandits have are serial killers but the dog you can get is stupid and locked behind one of the worst quests I've ever played. Normal quests are a drag making you talk to 6 different people between 6 different cities just to be told to now talk to an assortment of different people again with no real payoff

Instead of a chosen hero, you are a historical peasant simulator. You will spend three real-world hours trying to learn how to read a single book, 20 minutes failing to pick a basic door lock, and your entire life savings on a bathhouse trip just so the local noblemen don't smell you coming from a mile away

Everything is great except the most important feature. COMPAT its so awful that I cant even begin to describe it. So buggy, not responsive, and designed for 1v1 only. The moment you have 2 npcs or more you're done for. Also it's such a great idea to give npc the ability to perfectly block everything you attack. You'd ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ spend 10mins in a fight and do absolutely no damage.

EDIT: Ok maybe it's not tooo bad, combat still annoying but manageable when you get used to it. Still it took me around 25hrs just to finally start having fun at combat

fighting well thought for duels & poorly implemented for every other scenario.

horse riding is good only on tracks, offroad there are ton of concrete bushes waiting for you to come too close and then unhorse you.

the main problem with this game is that it is barely a rpg game, your henry even if you choose different options in dialogues based on your stats will always treat some person only like the devs designed, so you cant be bad person, you can only act as one with few characters. good examples of how lacking the game is in terms of freedom are people of merhojed, sebastian vom berg, zach and florian. if the game really doesn't let you threaten them to push the plot forward without some extra annoying steps that could be seen as clever only if you are playing for first time.

quests are poorly made, some are timed others are not. it would be much better experience if all were timed or none 

the worst thing about this game is the fact that the world revolves around you, like nothing happens in this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ if you just spend 100 days just by stealing cabbages from rattay and sleeping whole day in mill
and even if you would spend those days killing everybody in every town still they will respawn and nothing will change.

However these are the only bad things but the game has a lot of good things to offer if you just play it the way devs intended.

I can’t fully recommend this game, but I also can’t say I regret playing it.

The story is genuinely great and the world feels realistic in a way most RPGs don’t. Some moments were amazing and I got really attached to the characters.

But the gameplay can be very slow, and the amount of talking sometimes felt endless. A lot of quests drag on for too long, and the game could definitely have been shorter. At times it felt more exhausting than fun.

Still, if you enjoy immersive medieval RPGs and don’t mind a very slow pace, there’s something special here.


That said, the gameplay is what ruined a lot of the experience for me. The combat system tries to be realistic, but instead it often feels clunky, frustrating, and exhausting. Fighting multiple enemies can become more annoying than challenging, and the controls never fully felt smooth or satisfying. Some mechanics also feel overly complicated just for the sake of realism, which slows the game down too much at times.

I can respect what the developers were trying to achieve, and I understand why many people love the game, but personally I wouldn’t recommend it to most players. If you care mainly about story, realism, and immersion, there’s a lot to appreciate. But if gameplay and combat are your main focus, the experience can become frustrating very quickly.

Overall, Kingdom Come: Deliverance has an excellent story and a unique world, but the gameplay made it difficult for me to fully enjoy the experience.

God i have so genuinely tried to like this game. I literally love everything about it, except... combat. I don't know if its just me, but i literally cannot grasp it. Even after mods and such, i just cannot stand the combat system. I've played all Dark Souls/Bloodborne/Elden Ring, have nearly 250+ hours on For Honor on Playstation, and this is the one game i had to put down purely due to the combat, which sucks. Not for me, personally. Lovely graphics and story though.

The game's save system completely ruins the fun. You do all these activities, play for minutes on end, but then you die because of a mistake or get stuck due to a glitch. When this happens, the game might load a save from half an hour or even an hour earlier. You might say you can save by drinking a potion from your inventory or by sleeping, but you don’t do that every time you’re exploring the open world. If it happens once, it’s no big deal, but if it happens repeatedly, the enjoyment of the game drops significantly.

Having just finished KCD2 and wanting to have a similar great experience with KCD1, this game is an absolute catastrophe. The best thing to say about this game is how impressive it is for the developers to have managed making KCD2 after this piece of s*. Holy mother of G.
Great Combat mechanics, love the story as a history enjoyer. Fills the space for a historical skyrim type game

I have now played both KCD and KCDII for over a month and a half and now that I have finished both I dont know what to do with my life, its like watching the best show you have ever seen on the regular and now its finished.  This is one, if not the greatest games I have ever played and urge you to buy it.

80%
+ Fantastic story and DLC
+ Unique experience
+ Great map design
+ In-depth combat
- Glitchy in places
- Voice acting inconsistent

I love open world, I love medieval, and this is the best of both. Yes... you can get your foot caught on a small rock now and then and get annoyed at the pathfinding that goes along with it, but it's still a brilliant game in my oppinion.

Bit rough around the edges and quite buggy at times.
Still in many aspects better than part II and definitely worth playing.

This game is a decent game, but it lacks quality of life updates.

It has a somewhat good story, and some of the quests were fun, like the one where you get drunk with Father Godwin, the quest where you become a monk for a while, or the one where you have to save an entire village from the plague. The main storyline is okayish, but it felt rushed toward the end and is obviously continued in the sequel, which I have yet to play.

The open world feels very empty. The NPCs do have a certain daily routine, but they have little to no meaningful dialogue. I was expecting more immersion, but it just isn't there. As a result, I rarely explored much because every area felt the same empty, empty, and empty.

[h2] Major Issues [/h2]

This is where the game gets frustrating and annoying.

First of all, there aren't any quicksave options. If you're far into a mission and die, you have to repeat everything you've done over and over again, which is incredibly annoying. I ended up downloading a third party mod just to add quick saving.

[h2] Worst Quest Markers Ever [/h2]

Many of the quest markers in this game are placed incorrectly, so you often have to look up videos online to find the exact location. This was annoying as hell.

[h2] Combat System [/h2]

I understand that they wanted to make it realistic, but personally, I found it difficult. You need to spend a lot of time getting used to it and improving as you play. I'm not a fan of it. Another major issue is that you can't heal during combat, especially when fighting large groups of enemies. As a result, you have to be extremely careful, since even one strong hit can cause bleeding and quickly put you at a disadvantage.

I'd give this game a solid 6.7/10.

The game has a engaging and entertaining story, along with this it has an intricate combat system and enjoyable game mechanics.

I would highly reccomend this to anyone intrested in a medival rpg with a world you can get lost in for hours.

Absolutely terrific game.  Although it does have it ups and downs with the large amount of fetch quests/ it has an enormous amount of content and i feel i have yet to scratch the surface. easily an 9/10 game for me.

i guess i would recommend this game. only the combat system is very frustrating to try and learn, more frustrating than anything I've played. it seems like it was designed for one on one combat and nothing else. in a skirmish where you've got an army of your own it's piss easy to just come in from the side and beat the ♥♥♥♥ out of whoever is fighting your teammates. it is incredibly difficult to walk away from any fight where you have to fight two or more people on your own, and you won't get away unscathed. your lock on will snap to other enemies when you don't want it to and you'll just be staring at an enemy who isn't even in melee range with another just to the side of you who you can't hit because of the lock on. 
maybe there is a way to turn off lock on and i just forgot, i don't know. but if you can, never ever have your lock on when fighting a whole crowed.
Game is nice, and you can easily put 1.000 hours in it, but the saving system, i understand they try to make it somehow "hard" but still.

Golden Brown - The strangles.
This game is the standard of the medieval RPG genre. Don't regret spending even a single groschen.

You are Henry of Skalitz, squire of Sir Radzig Kobyla. You start off as some lazy schmucko who is destined for the forge, until some bald ♥♥♥♥ said "Nah" and turned your world upside down.

After going through every DLC, the full story, and screaming at the very bugged sidequest "A Bird in the Hand" where you have to find nightingales in the forest entirely based on sound, you return only to find out the bird traps sunk to the ground, and you can never finish the quest. So I did everything else instead.

I absolutely love the progression in this game, for instance. You got to keep in mind that you start off as a village nobody with a very brief training with a swordsman to someone who is genuinely good with a sword. You won't reach the level of swordmaster in this game, though, because by the time you reach the highest levels of warfare and the sword, you will just slightly more skilled than professional soldiers turned to banditry. They all got the same skills as you and are able to do master strikes and undefendable combos like you do. While I can appreciate that, it eventually gets tedious that there's quite a plateau in skill eventually.

One more thing though. How can some common peasant bandit know exceptional combat knowledge like you do? How the hell can he master strike me with a ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ tree branch he calls a club? And how do those losers end up overpowering a knight (me) who's 3 times as skilled as they are? Some of the balancing here should've definitely been done better.

The story itself starts out great, where a seemingly unexpected source have to live a completely different life after a brief adventure in the prologue. But then, some range from the exceptionally tedious and the shockingly buggy as well [spoiler] (Looking at you, Needle in a Haystack). In it, You disguise yourself as a benedictine monk, and have to live a diligent life as a scribe and potion maker. And you're under a very strict schedule that if you stray even slightly outside of the timezones, you'll find yourself getting accosted by your brothers. I WAS ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ POISONED AND LAYING ON THE FLOOR FOAMING FROM THE MOUTH, WHAT DO YOU MEAN I'M SKIPPING MY DUTIES?! I GET THIS IS SUPPOSED TO BE VERY HISTORICALLY ACCURATE, BUT YOU MEAN TO TELL ME ST. BENEDICT DOESN'T GIVE A DAMN ABOUT SOMEONE PASSED OUT ON THE FLOOR?[/spoiler]

The fact of the matter that I should point out is that I was so excited when the story finally came to an end after slogging through the last parts. While it has some high points, a lot of the quests were full of lows after [spoiler] you killed Runt.[/spoiler] And honestly, that just felt as a great disappointment.

6/10 I loved the game, but a lot of the exceptionally and overly realistic parts can really get to you.
Combat is tricky to get hold of at first but after you've mastered it this game is so much fun. Good story as well
Лепшая гульня пра сярэднявечча на маёй памяці. Такой атмасферы, такіх блізкіх майму сэрцу краявідаў не памятаю нідзе. Цудоўнейшы праект, 10 шнапсаў з 10!

This game is so peak. 
The combat system is really good when you get a hang of it and it's very realistic, as is most of the aspect of the game. Love the humor at times and although the start is a bit annoying after the first 10 hours it gets you really hooked and invested.

Fantastic, ambitious and at times frustrating

KCD is a game that more than once, within the first 5 hours, I was thinking about uninstalling due to bugs and other various mechanics that had produced frustration. I am so glad I pushed through that and ended up basically playing the whole game and all DLC through. 

KCD does some very important things extremely well (for a small studio especially!) 
1) Immersion & actual Role Playing in an RPG.
2) A historical RPG that makes a really good attempt to throw you into a place and time in history.

The journey you go on with Henry is well crafted, from a Blacksmith's son without much knowledge of the world you're given freedom to steadily build up your skills in various areas from Reading & Lockpicking to Sword fighting & Drinking. There is a very steady feeling of progress that feels satisfying, especially as many "silly" or minor skills can prove booms to other critical skills. Your little slice of Medieval Bohemia is highly responsive to who you are and your various deeds.  People remember if you helped or hindered them and when they pop up in later quests or events will act in appropriate ways. As your station in the world & the equipment you wear improves, people start to respond in a totally different manner to you. The designers also clearly made a real effort to not just make Henry a modern bloke or a generic fantasy protagonist. You're surrounded by a really well realised cast or characters who push & mentor you in your (long!) journey with them. This game has a fabulous sense of humour about it and fully takes advantage of the setting and our hayseed protagonist. 

Speaking of Medieval Bohemia, the environments in this game are stunning. Often when I am walking through woods I think "why cant games actually capture this environment" KCD does this with aplomb. Simply wandering around the woods to explore, track down bandits or hunt deer is a treat. Scale is something KCD does so well. You are in a small part of a larger kingdom, which is part of a much larger world. You learn the environments and settlements, there is a cast of characters with you on your journey. There is no hero of destiny or massive battles for the fate of the world but it all works so well. 

This game will frustrate you at times. I fell through the floor and lost 35 minutes progress (you cannot just save any time). The combat swerves between being mostly fantastic to frustrating ("Masterstrikes" a poor design decision). In it's ambition this game bites off more than it can chew, mechanics fail during certain  quests or you get caught stealing through walls or floors. Overall I loved the" realism" mechanics but some of them become either tedious or silly at points (Repairs, cleanliness etc).

If you are willing to put the time in and commit to this lovingly crafted historical RPG it will reward you richly. The journey I went on with Henry will not be one I forget easily. The moments just lost in the beautiful countryside, the desperate fights with a handful of bandits on the road or spending hours living the lift of a medieval monk. From blacksmith's boy to the concluding scene. Epic.
better than skyrim, amazing game, super fun
This game will piss you off but it is amazing. Wish I started it sooner, and could not be more excited for warhorse to do their middle earth game.
its a good game, but the combat is horrific. I tried to learn but no, it is just unfun garbage.  Was able to play and enjoy myself after i installed some mods
haven't played such an immersive open-world storyline game in a long time! One of the best so far!

Fantastic series, I love this game, I started with KCD2 and liked it so much I had to go back and see the rest of the story.  There is more polish on KCD2 as you would expect, but I am really really loving the storytelling and game.

When I read about this game I thought "Great idea, but that much grinding is not for me".
There is actually not that much grinding while still keeping a great fantasy of "realism". Like, yes, we know you don't learn how to read because of one quest, but still the concept of not everyone reading and you having to learn is pretty amazing.
I jumped into this game when it was 5usd but I would pay full price no problem for something like this. Already buying the second entry in the series!

Costing a long time to finish this game from 2019 to 2026. At first watch clips of this game, I surprise with much terms in this game to dress borsimia in 15th centery. But I found the battle system it is hard to learn. That's the reason why I gave up in 2019.  Herry need exercise more before some tasks. If your have enough patient to finish such many boring missions, You will get joyful trip in Borsimia

Let me preface this review by saying - KINGDOM COME DELIVERANCE IS NOT FOR EVERYONE. 

I mean it. Right at the beginning of game, I was seriously considering to quit and refund it. For real. Because everything is so hard. You can't fight, you can't run fast, you can barely ride a horse. 

But, you see. Things do get better in the game... if you're willing to be patient. Kingdom Come Deliverance is the embodiment of an interactive slowburn video game epic. 

What made me stay playing it for more than 190 hours is the rich storytelling. Henry is basically starting from zero and he can be a hero too (or an evil bastard), which depends on your choice. Yet this isn't just your average revenge story - there's more to this. Drama. Horror. Violence. Comedy. You feel every facet of emotion while playing the main and side quests. Also, don't forget to play the DLC especially A Woman's Lot! Playing as Theresa restored my faith to the game and gave me a reason to power through to the end.

All in all, the game is an absolute gem if you have already enjoyed Red Dead Redemption 2 or The Witcher 3. Yeah, I put this game at par with Rockstar Studios and CD Projekt Red's games because Warhorse Studios is just *THAT* good. The only downside is the brutal combat mechanics - good things the developer actually make the combat easier in Kingdom Come Deliverance 2.
Compelling story, interesting world. Enjoying it so far. Have ran into a few bugs that made me have to reload a slightly older save. Not a huge deal. Would still recommend.

# A Flawed Masterpiece: Kingdom Come: Deliverance's Ambitious Medieval Vision

## Summary

After 20 hours immersed in the muddy fields and bustling towns of 14th-century Bohemia, Kingdom Come: Deliverance stands as one of gaming's most ambitious and rewarding experiences—despite its technical shortcomings. This is a game that refuses to compromise on its vision of historical authenticity and immersive roleplay, delivering a medieval RPG that feels genuinely alive. While the CryEngine 3 struggles to keep pace with the game's ambitions, the exceptional storytelling, innovative mechanics, and memorable characters make this a must-play for dedicated RPG enthusiasts willing to tolerate performance compromises.

## Detailed Gameplay Analysis

**Immersive Roleplay & Narrative Excellence**

Kingdom Come: Deliverance shatters the traditional RPG formula by grounding itself in historical reality rather than fantasy tropes. The story, rooted in Polish medieval history, unfolds with remarkable authenticity and emotional weight. Your character, Henry, isn't a chosen one—he's a blacksmith's son caught in extraordinary circumstances. This grounded narrative approach creates genuine investment in the world and its inhabitants. The dialog system deserves particular praise; conversations feel natural and consequential, with NPCs remembering your actions and treating you accordingly based on your reputation and social standing.

**Revolutionary Combat System**

The sword fighting mechanics represent a genuine innovation in RPG design. Rather than abstracted button-mashing, combat demands positioning, timing, and directional awareness. Each strike must be aimed, and blocking requires precision. This creates a learning curve that's steep but incredibly rewarding—once you master the system, combat encounters feel like genuine duels rather than stat-checking exercises. The first-person perspective intensifies this immersion, making every encounter feel personal and dangerous.

**Systemic Depth**

Beyond combat, the game excels in its interconnected systems. Your character needs to eat, sleep, and maintain hygiene. NPCs follow daily routines, creating a living world that continues whether you're present or not. Lockpicking, pickpocketing, and alchemy all require genuine skill rather than stat checks. This systemic approach means there are often multiple solutions to problems, rewarding creative thinking and exploration.

## Technical Performance Evaluation

This is where Kingdom Come: Deliverance stumbles significantly. Despite your RTX 5060 Ti being a capable mid-range card, the CryEngine 3 implementation struggles to maintain consistent performance. Frame rate dips are noticeable during populated areas, and the engine shows its age with occasional stuttering and optimization issues. The game demands substantial CPU resources alongside GPU power, which can create bottlenecks on systems that don't meet the higher-end specifications.

**Performance Reality Check:**
- Expect 60fps at high settings only on premium hardware
- Mid-range systems will require compromises between visual fidelity and frame rate
- Loading times are lengthy, though this is somewhat mitigated by the immersive world design
- Occasional bugs and crashes, though patches have improved stability significantly since launch

The irony is that the graphics, while occasionally stunning, don't always justify the performance cost. The game prioritizes visual detail and draw distance over optimization—a trade-off that won't appeal to everyone.

## Value for Money: 10/10

Despite technical limitations, the value proposition is exceptional. At its current price point (often discounted), you're receiving 40-100+ hours of content depending on playstyle. The depth of mechanics, quality of writing, and sheer ambition justify the investment. Few games offer this level of immersion and roleplay freedom. For players willing to engage with the game's systems rather than rush through it, the value is genuinely outstanding.

## Pros & Cons

**Strengths:**
- Exceptional narrative grounded in historical authenticity
- Revolutionary combat system that rewards skill and practice
- Impressive dialog system with meaningful NPC interactions
- Wonderful graphics that create an immersive medieval world
- Memorable characters with genuine personality and agency
- Remarkable mechanical innovation for a 2018 title
- Systemic depth that encourages creative problem-solving

**Weaknesses:**
- Significant performance optimization issues on mid-range hardware
- Steep learning curve may frustrate casual players
- CryEngine 3 shows its age and struggles with the game's ambitions
- Lengthy loading times
- Some bugs persist despite patches
- Not designed for players seeking action-focused gameplay

## Recommendation

**Rating: 8.5/10**

Kingdom Come: Deliverance is essential for hardcore RPG enthusiasts, historical fiction fans, and players who value immersion and mechanical depth over graphical polish and performance consistency. This is a game that rewards patience, curiosity, and engagement with its systems.

**Who Should Buy:**
- Dedicated RPG players seeking something genuinely different
- History enthusiasts interested in medieval authenticity
- Players with high-end hardware willing to optimize settings
- Those who value narrative and immersion over technical perfection

**Who Should Avoid:**
- Casual gamers seeking pick-up-and-play experiences
- Players with budget hardware or low performance tolerance
- Those expecting traditional fantasy RPG tropes
- Anyone prioritizing smooth 60fps performance

**Final Verdict:** Kingdom Come: Deliverance is a flawed masterpiece that dares to be different. Yes, the technical performance is frustrating, and yes, it demands patience and investment. But for those willing to meet it halfway, you'll discover one of gaming's most rewarding and authentic RPG experiences. The 20 hours I've invested have been genuinely memorable, and I'm eager to continue exploring Bohemia despite the performance compromises. This is a game that proves ambition and innovation can overcome technical limitations—barely, but meaningfully.

**Strongly Recommended for the Right Audience** ✓

A great game, but at the same time a buggy mess
The horrible : because of a bug i can't advance further in the game, Hans won't talk with me right after i woke up from the hunt or even when i waited he still didn't talk and just kept repeating the same lines that meshed on one another, also in the save that i made after waking up i got food poisoning, alright not a big deal, i reloaded the save that i made before food poisoning, and the food poisoning was still there and it was the last save i could load, so now i'm softlocked because of a stupid bug
The good : From this horrible bug aside, the game is unique and amazing, i love that food can spoil, and i love that this game throws in the trash the "chosen one" , i love the combat, i love the story, i love the characters, but unfortunatley my journey came o an end because of a bug

[h3] Great story and characters [/h3]
I'm a big fan of history-based games, and this one is a gem. You feel like living through the events without being overloaded with annoying simulation mechanics. The developers have discovered a middle ground. 

Still, the part I enjoyed the most is the story and characters. They are bold and memorable. The story is captivating, and your choices matter. 

On top of that, the game allows you to tailor your own play style that affects the world around you as well as how the story progresses. This allows for replayability. Awesome game! Don't miss out!

Absolutely amazing! 

     I've heard of this game for years and thought I wasn't going to enjoy it or think it was too challenging and quit early, but in fact quite the opposite happened. I sank hours into this game and loved every minute of it. From entering and exploring shops, churches, and towns to getting into melee fights with Cumans, bandits and other ruffians. The combat is challenging at first and when you get out-numbered you're basically cooked, but once you have the right weapons, shield and armour, combat will be easier to handle. 

     Henry's story was absolutely great from start to finish. (It's a shame Warhorse studios got bought out by Embracer group and Blackrock) But the original KCD is one of the best open world games I've ever played and the historical accuracy that was put in, made all the difference, cuz this isn't your average fantasy RPG game. It actually has no elements of fantasy at all, just pure historical setting which I love. (You can still pull off some Elder-scrolls type shenanigans tho)   

      So, if you have been sleeping on KCD, give the original a try!

And also Jesus Christ be praised!
fantastic time playing, with a few bumps along the way. worth ever cent, can't wait to play the second game!

KCD is honestly one of the best games I've played in recent years. What I mean by that is, it's a game that has made the biggest impression to me in a while. It pulled me in. I've been properly invested in this game and its story and at the time of writing, I've just finished it's main story and I'm going to check out the DLC.

The story is VERY good and did not end in the way I expected it to - which was a nice surprise. Very well written all the way through; you're in good hands here.

Before I jumped in, I read a lot  of negatives about the combat system. To my surprise, I ended up liking it. It's fun when you get the hang of it. My advice would be to not sleep on it. As soon as you can train with Captain Bernard, slug it out with him for a few days. Once you've done that, you'll beat every 1 on 1 fight with no problem.

There are a lot of simulation elements to the game which took me some getting used to. Alchemy for example is a bit of a protracted process which had me going "wait, is this for real?" when I first encountered it. It's totally for real.

You'll also need to brew or buy a potion to save the game, which is probably the only thing I disliked as I felt that isn't very player considerate. 

Very early on into playing I went and downloaded some mods to make things smoother. I used these:
- Easy to see herbs
- Instant herb picking (trust me you'll want this one)
- Instant potion crafting (after you've learned and brewed one, you can brew them with a single button)
- Stay clean longer get dirty gradually
- Unlimited saving

Some folks will enjoy the harder / simulation-ish edge of the base game, but these made the game a dream for me.

KCD is a GREAT game. I'm so glad I decided to pick it up.
Nice and evolving game. At first it is very difficult, but give a chance and you feel the medieval vibe.
combat is kinda hard or at least has a steep learning curve but the story is cool and overall its a nice game.

Would definitely recommend giving this game a try if you enjoy RPGs in any capacity. My only gripe is that combat's a bit clunky in the beginning and not super player friendly imo. You'll be given the opportunity to train with certain individuals (such as Captains and others). I'd recommend taking the time to do it bc once the combat clicked for me it was so much fun. It's less of "get good" by practicing over and over and over, and more of simply understanding the rhythm and timing of swordplay. But man, once I got it that made the game so much more fun than it already was.

This is one of the best RPG's in the last decade imo. The story is engaging and there are so many cool elements, from the codex that has very detailed information about the locations that were real back in the day, to the dice mini game that I can spend hours at gambling against other townsfolk. The combat is difficult but fun. I would say this feels like a Elder Scrolls inspired game but unlike the failures of Bethesda lately this is a refreshing take on a medieval RPG

Truly an [b]RPG game[/b]. There is jank, bugs and weird issues here and there but for the most part, the roleplay aspect of the game feels solid and fun. Joining a tournament very drunk, running around in villages and spreading lies and doing inexplicable things to terrify the villagers, fighting in heroic sieges, going around asking questions in the name of your lord like a medieval detective... It is all there. 

It tends to get slightly boring after overplaying it due to nature of real life requirements such as eating, cleaning, shopping, running errands, traveling with heavy objects (meat, skin, etc) but that's life I guess. 

I'll give it [b]9/10[/b] but the -1 comes from engine/software related issues and bugs long unresolved. I hope they fixed them in the 2nd game :)
Great storyline, holds up years later, well worth to play before kcd2

I bought the pack with kcd2 and a DLC, and I can already tell I’ll be playing this impressive game for a long time.

Amazing medieval RPG with a great story, immersive world, and unique combat system. It can be difficult at first, but once you learn the mechanics, the game becomes incredibly rewarding.

10/10 – one of the most immersive RPGs ever made.⚔️🍺

SO far the story and dialogue seem really good, but he actual gameplay is terrible. The combat is slow and awkward. Riding a horse is counter-intuitive. There is no easy way to save or restart the game. If they eliminated all gameplay elements and cut the price in half it seems like this would be a decent graphic novel, but as a game, it's just a string of too-long fetch quests. I've been playing for just over two hours and I'm debating whether or not to just stop and delete it from my library. How much irritation is the story worth? Right now, I'm not having fun. There are a lot of good reviews, so I'll give it a little more time.
     OK.I have now played for 5-1/2 hours and can definitively say this game is a waste of time. Don't buy it. even if it's on sale. The gameplay and controls are so bad they completely ruin what would otherwise be a decent story. I stopped after the 4th quest and uninstalled it to save space.
Game is hell on earth but it keeps me coming back in the hopes i will one day be good and complete the game
This is amazing this the best RPG I have played, it really put role playing into the RPG. 100% recommended
